elements.sent-2411-001: Model-based reinforcement learning refers to a set of approaches capable of sample-efficient decision making, which create an explicit model of the environment.
elements.sent-2411-002: This model can subsequently be used for learning optimal policies.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this paper, we propose a temporal Gaussian Mixture Model composed of a perception model and a transition model.
elements.sent-2411-004: The perception model extracts discrete (latent) states from continuous observations using a variational Gaussian mixture likelihood.
elements.sent-2411-005: Importantly, our model constantly monitors the collected data searching for new Gaussian components, i.e., the perception model performs a form of structure learning \citep{structure_learning_AI,friston2018bayesian,neacsu2022structure as it learns the number of Gaussian components in the mixture.}
elements.sent-2411-006: Additionally, the transition model learns the temporal transition between consecutive time steps by taking advantage of the Dirichlet-categorical conjugacy.
elements.sent-2411-007: Both the perception and transition models are able to forget part of the data points, while integrating the information they provide within the prior, which ensure fast variational inference.
elements.sent-2411-008: Finally, decision making is performed with a variant of Q-learning which is able to learn Q-values from beliefs over states.
elements.sent-2411-009: Empirically, we have demonstrated the model's ability to learn the structure of several mazes: the model discovered the number of states and the transition probabilities between these states.
elements.sent-2411-010: Moreover, using its learned Q-values, the agent was able to successfully navigate from the starting position to the maze's exit.
elements.sent-2411-011: Model-based reinforcement learning is a theory describing how an agent should interact with its environment.
elements.sent-2411-012: More precisely, the agent maintains a model of its environment, which is composed of observations, states, and actions.
elements.sent-2411-013: When new observations become available, the agent needs to estimate the most likely states of the environment.
elements.sent-2411-014: This process is generally referred to as perception or inference, and can be implemented by minimizing the variational free energy, also known as the negative evidence lower bound in machine learning \citep{blei2017variational .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Variational inference is a form of approximate inference, where the true posterior is approximated by the variational distribution.
elements.sent-2411-016: Specifically, inference is generally rendered tractable by restricting the approximate posterior to a class of distributions, where each distribution corresponds to a different set of parameter values.
elements.sent-2411-017: Inference then refers to the optimization of these parameters such that the variational free energy is minimized.
elements.sent-2411-018: An important family of distributions frequently used in variational inference is the exponential family \citep{holland1981exponential , which contains most of the well-known distributions.}
elements.sent-2411-019: For example, the Gaussian, Bernoulli, and Dirichlet distributions are all members of this family.
elements.sent-2411-020: Importantly, all the distributions within the exponential family can be mapped to the same functional form, which can be used to derive modular inference algorithms such as variational message passing \citep{VMP_TUTO .}
elements.sent-2411-021: In these previous approaches, the generative model's structure is known in advance, i.e., the number of latent states is known by the agent.
elements.sent-2411-022: In practice, this means that the model needs to be designed by a domain expert and is not learned by the agent.
elements.sent-2411-023: However, for some applications, the model may not be available, or experts may only have a partial understanding of the problem.
elements.sent-2411-024: In this case, learning the model becomes essential.
elements.sent-2411-025: Recent work has focused on problems where observations are discrete \citep{structure_learning_AI,friston2018bayesian,neacsu2022structure .}
elements.sent-2411-026: In contrast, this paper addresses the case where observations are continuous.
elements.sent-2411-027: Throughout this paper, we assume that the data is distributed according to a mixture of multivariate Gaussian distributions.
elements.sent-2411-028: Then, each component of the mixture is associated with a latent state, and we aim to learn how these states change over time.
elements.sent-2411-029: The key challenge is to identify the correct number of components.
elements.sent-2411-030: This requires the model to add and remove components when necessary.
elements.sent-2411-031: While the Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) is able to prune superfluous components, we also need a way to increase the number of components as new clusters of data points are discovered.
elements.sent-2411-032: To this end, our agent constantly monitors the data for new clusters of data points, and new Gaussian components are added to the mixtures when such clusters are identified.
elements.sent-2411-033: While learning the model structure and inferring the current state using variational inference is useful, it does not prescribe which actions should be taken.
elements.sent-2411-034: Thus, the next step consists of comparing the quality of different policies, where in reinforcement learning, the quality of a policy is the discounted sum of rewards.
elements.sent-2411-035: Ideally, we would like to compare all possible policies, and select the policy with the highest quality.
elements.sent-2411-036: Unfortunately, the number of valid policies grows exponentially with the time horizon of planning.
elements.sent-2411-037: Specifically, if the agent can choose one of $A$ actions, for $T$ time steps, then the number of possible policies is: $A^T$.
elements.sent-2411-038: This exponential explosion renders an exhaustive search intractable.
elements.sent-2411-039: Instead, algorithms such as Monte Carlo tree search \citep{browne2012survey can be used to efficiently explore the space of valid policies \citep{BTAI_empirical,CHAMPION2022295,BTAI_3MF,BTAI_BF .}}
elements.sent-2411-040: An alternative approach is the Q-learning algorithm \citep{Mnih2015 , where instead of searching the space of policies using a search tree, the agent learns the value of performing each action in each state.}
elements.sent-2411-041: However, Q-learning requires states to be observable.
elements.sent-2411-042: Therefore, we propose a variant of the Q-learning algorithm which can learn from beliefs over states.
elements.sent-2411-043: In section \ref{sec:perception , we present the variational Gaussian mixture model, which is used to learn the number of components and perform inference of the latent variables.}
elements.sent-2411-044: Section \ref{sec:transition explains how the temporal transition can be learned by taking advantage of a categorical-Dirichlet model.}
elements.sent-2411-045: Then, in Section \ref{sec:forgetting , we describe an optimization which allows the agent to forget part of the data points by integrating the information they provided into the prior.}
elements.sent-2411-046: By forgetting part of the data, the agent reduces the amount of memory required, while speeding up the inference process.
elements.sent-2411-047: Next, in Section \ref{sec:planning , we describe how Q-learning can be adapted to work with beliefs over states.}
elements.sent-2411-048: Finally, in Section \ref{sec:experiments we empirically validate our approach, before summarizing and concluding the paper in Section \ref{sec:conclusion .}}
elements.sent-2411-049: In this section, we discuss the theory and implementation details of our perception model.
elements.sent-2411-050: Specifically, we describe how mean shift clustering can be used to initialize the parameters of a variational Gaussian mixture (VGM) model.
elements.sent-2411-051: Then, we describe the VGM generative model, variational distribution and update equations.
elements.sent-2411-052: Given a dataset $\bm{X = \{\bm{x _1, ..., \bm{x _N\}$, the mean shift algorithm \citep{carreira2015review aims to find clusters of data points.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-053: While the mean shift algorithm does not require the number of clusters to be known, the user must specify the bandwidth parameter $\theta_b$, which defines the window's radius (see below).
elements.sent-2411-054: For each data point $\bm{x _n$, the mean shift algorithm initializes a spherical window of radius $\theta_b$ centred at $\bm{x _n$, then the window's centroid $\bm{m _n$ is set to the mean of the data points within the window, i.e., \begin{equation}m_n = \frac{1}{|W_{\theta_b}|} \sum_{n \in W_{\theta_b}} x_n, \label{eq:mean-shift}\end{equation} where $\mathcal{W _{\theta_b}$ is the set of data point indices within the window of radius $\theta_b$, and $|\mathcal{W _{\theta_b |$ corresponds to the number of data points in this window.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-055: The above update equation is iterated until convergence of the window's centroid position, at which point the window is centred on a part of the space with high density.
elements.sent-2411-056: The points whose window's centroid land close to each other are then grouped within the same cluster.
elements.sent-2411-057: This procedure will output $K$ clusters, we let $\bm{\hat{r _{nk}$ be one if the $n$-th data point is associated to the $k$-th cluster, and zero otherwise.}}
elements.sent-2411-058: Figure \ref{fig:k_means illustrates the mean shift algorithm.}

elements.sent-2411-059: While the mean-shift algorithm can identify clusters of data points, this algorithm does not scale well with the number of data points, i.e., for each additional data point Equation \eqref{eq:mean-shift must be iterated until convergence.}
elements.sent-2411-060: Thus, we need to find a way to discard data points without losing the information they contain.
elements.sent-2411-061: As will become apparent later, this can be achieved using a variational Gaussian mixture (VGM) model \citep{bishop2006pattern .}
elements.sent-2411-062: In the following sub-sections, we discuss the generative model, variational distribution, variational free energy and update equations of the variational Gaussian mixture.
elements.sent-2411-063: The generative model assumes that each data point $\bm{x _n$ is sampled from one of $K$ Gaussian components.}
elements.sent-2411-064: For each data point, $\bm{z _{nk}$ is a binary random variable equal to one if the $k$-th component is responsible for the $n$-th data point, and zero otherwise.}
elements.sent-2411-065: We denote by $\bm{z _{n}$ the one-of-$K$ (binary) random vector (i.e., a vector containing binary random variables) describing which component is responsible for the $n$-th data point, and let $\bm{Z = \{\bm{z _1, ..., \bm{z _N\}$ be the set of random vectors associated with all the data points.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-066: Additionally, each Gaussian component is parameterized by its mean vector $\bm{\mu _k$ and precision matrix $\bm{\Lambda _k$.}}
elements.sent-2411-067: For conciseness, the set of all mean vectors and precision matrices are denoted by $\bm{\mu}$ and $\bm{\Lambda}$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-068: Using the aforementioned notation, the probability of the data (i.e., $\bm{X}$), given the latent variables (i.e., $\bm{Z}$) and the component parameters (i.e., $\bm{\mu}$ and $\bm{\Lambda}$) is: \begin{equation}P(X | Z, \mu, \Lambda) = \prod_{n=1}^N \prod_{k=1}^K N(x_n; \mu_k, \Lambda_k^{-1})^{z_{nk}}. \label{eq:likelihood_factor}\end{equation} Moreover, the prior over latent variables is defined as a categorical distribution parameterized by the mixing coefficients $\bm{D}$, where $\bm{D _k$ is the fraction of the data for which the $k$-th component is responsible, i.e., the larger $\bm{D _k$ is, the more prevalent the $k$-th component will be.}}
elements.sent-2411-069: More formally: \begin{equation}P(Z | D) = \prod_{n=1}^N Cat(z_n ; D). \label{eq:Z_prior}\end{equation} Finally, the VGM model takes advantage of conjugate priors by defining the prior over $\bm{D}$, $\bm{\mu}$ and $\bm{\Lambda}$ as a Dirichlet, Gaussian and Wishart distributions, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-070: More precisely: \begin{equation}P(\mu | \Lambda) = \prod_{k=1}^K N(\mu_k; m_k, (\beta_k\Lambda_k)^{-1}), \label{eq:mu_prior} P(\Lambda) = \prod_{k=1}^K W(\Lambda_k; W_k, v_k), \label{eq:lambda_prior} P(D) = Dir(D; d), \phantom{\prod^K} \label{eq:D_prior}\end{equation} where $d$ is a vector containing concentration parameters of the prior over $\bm{D}$, $\bm{W _k$ and $v_k$ are the scale matrix and degrees of freedom of the Wishart distribution over $\bm{\Lambda _k$, $\bm{m _k$ is the mean vector of the prior over $\bm{\mu _k$, and finally, $\beta_k$ is a parameter scaling the values of the precision matrix $\bm{\Lambda _k$.}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-110: In this section, we explain how to learn the transition mapping between consecutive time steps using a categorical-Dirichlet model.
elements.sent-2411-111: When combined with the perception model from the last section, the resulting model is a temporal Gaussian mixture (TGM) that is able to learn the environment's structure.
elements.sent-2411-112: That is, a model which can learn the number of states and the transition probabilities between states given the action taken.
elements.sent-2411-113: While the variational Gaussian mixture (from Section \ref{ssec:gm ) is able to learn a static model of data, it cannot model time series.}
elements.sent-2411-114: A potential solution would be to duplicate the Gaussian mixture likelihood at each time step, and to define the transition probability between any two consecutive time steps, c.f., Figure \ref{fig:tgm_gm .}
elements.sent-2411-115: Unfortunately, as depicted in Figure \ref{fig:tgm , adding such transition mapping interferes with the VGM's ability to learn one component for each cluster of data points.}

elements.section-2411-004: To forget or not to forget
elements.sent-2411-152: In the previous sections, we described the perception and transition models.
elements.sent-2411-153: Importantly, we explained that each model is provided with two sets of data points, i.e., the data points that can be forgotten and the data points that need to be kept in memory.
elements.sent-2411-154: The forgettable data points are used to compute empirical priors, i.e., posterior distributions taking into account only the data points to forget.
elements.sent-2411-155: Importantly, empirical priors have the same functional form as the prior distributions and can therefore be used as prior beliefs when the inference process is over, effectively integrating the information provided by the forgettable data points into the prior.
elements.sent-2411-156: We now focus on how to decide which data points to forget and keep.
elements.sent-2411-157: When learning, the brain needs to be plastic by allowing changes in synaptic connectivity, as well as in the strength of existing synaptic connections.
elements.sent-2411-158: However, these changes should ideally preserve the previously learned concepts to ensure some form of stability over time, and avoid forgetting useful information.
elements.sent-2411-159: This is the plasticity versus stability dilemma \citep{10.3389/fpsyg.2013.00504 .}
elements.sent-2411-160: Our model is faced with a similar challenge, as in general, new components are progressively discovered as the agent explores the environment.
elements.sent-2411-161: These new components need to be learned without forgetting older components.
elements.sent-2411-162: For example, imagine a mouse moving through a maze and observing a noisy estimate of its position.
elements.sent-2411-163: As new maze cells are explored, new positions are sampled and new components will become visible.
elements.sent-2411-164: However, if the number of samples for a new component is too small, this component may be confused with nearby cells, until enough data points are available to differentiate between the cells.
elements.sent-2411-165: In this section, we discuss how our model addresses the plasticity vs stability dilemma.
elements.sent-2411-166: More precisely, we assume that the Gaussian components can be either flexible (plastic) or fixed (stable).
elements.sent-2411-167: All components are initialized flexibly, and they become fixed if they persist over long periods of time.
elements.sent-2411-168: Figure \ref{fig:flexible_and_fixed_components illustrates how components transform into fixed components.}
elements.sent-2411-169: Mathematically, every 100 training iterations, the variational Gaussian mixture identifies a set of Gaussian components $\mathcal{G _t$, where $t \in \{100, 200, ...\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-170: Given two Gaussian components with distributions $\mathcal{N (X; \bm{\mu _1, \bm{\Lambda _1) \in \mathcal{G _t$ and $\mathcal{N (X; \bm{\mu _2, \bm{\Lambda _2) \in \mathcal{G _{t+100}$, we say that $\mathcal{N (X; \bm{\mu _1, \bm{\Lambda _1)$ at time $t$ becomes $\mathcal{N (X; \bm{\mu _2, \bm{\Lambda _2)$ at time $t+100$, if: \begin{equation}\kl{N(X; \mu_1, \Lambda_1)}{N(X; \mu_2, \Lambda_2)} < \theta_{kl},\end{equation} where $\theta_{\text{kl }$ is the threshold under which two Gaussian components are considered identical, and in our simulation $\theta_{\text{kl }$ was set to 0.5.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-171: In other words, a Gaussian component $\mathcal{N (X; \bm{\mu _1, \bm{\Lambda _1) \in \mathcal{G _t$ persists over time if there exists another component $\mathcal{N (X; \bm{\mu _2, \bm{\Lambda _2) \in \mathcal{G _{t+100}$ for which the KL-divergence is smaller than a predetermined threshold.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-172: If a Gaussian component persists more than $\theta_{\text{counts }$ times in a row, the component becomes fixed.}

The number associated to each component counts how many times a component has been observed. When a component has been observed five times in a row or more, this component becomes fixed and is therefore depicted in black. In contrast, red components are flexible components.} \label{fig:flexible_and_fixed_components}
elements.subsection-2411-006: Which data points should be forgotten?
elements.sent-2411-174: Figure \ref{fig:forgettable_data_points illustrates the data available for a trial composed of seven actions and eight observations.}
elements.sent-2411-175: For each observation, the variational Gaussian mixture can be used to compute the associated responsibilities.
elements.sent-2411-176: An observation $x_t$ can be forgotten if the previous $x_{t-1}$, current $x_t$, and next $x_{t+1}$ observations are all associated to a fixed component.
elements.sent-2411-177: Of course, if the observation is at the beginning of a trial, then the previous observation can be ignored.
elements.sent-2411-178: Similarly, if the observation is at the end of a trial, then the next observation can be ignored.
elements.sent-2411-179: Following these rules in Figure \ref{fig:forgettable_data_points , the observations $x_0$, $x_4$ and $x_8$ can be forgotten.}
elements.sent-2411-181: If we forget these data points, we obtain the dataset illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:after_forgeting_data_points .}
elements.sent-2411-182: This sheds some light on the triplets ($a_t$, $r_t$, $r_{t+1}$) that will become unavailable, and that must therefore be added to the forgetting set of the transition model.
elements.sent-2411-184: Thus, for example, the triplet ($a_7$, $r_7$, $r_8$) can be forgotten, which reflects the fact that $7 \in M^{'}$.
elements.sent-2411-185: In this section, we explain how Q-learning \citep{sutton2018reinforcement can be adapted to work with a problem where there is uncertainty about the current states.}
elements.sent-2411-186: First, we introduce the standard Q-learning algorithm, then we adapt this algorithm to work with beliefs over states.
elements.sent-2411-187: The Q-learning algorithm aims to solve the reinforcement learning problem, where at each time step, an agent takes an action $a_t$ based on the current state $s_t$.
elements.sent-2411-188: Then, the environment produces a next state $s_{t+1}$ and the reward $r_{t+1}$ obtained by taking action $a_t$ in state $s_t$.
elements.sent-2411-189: The agent's goal is to maximize the discounted sum of future rewards: \begin{equation}G_t = \sum_{\tau = t + 1}^{T} \gamma^{\tau - t - 1} r_{\tau}, \label{eq:return_definit}\end{equation} where $\gamma \in [0, 1]$ is the discount factor, and the time horizon $T$ must be larger than $t + 1$.
elements.sent-2411-190: In reinforcement learning, a policy $\pi$ defines the probability of taking each action in each state, i.e., $\pi(a|s)$.
elements.sent-2411-191: The expected value of a state $s$ under a policy $\pi$ is denoted $v_{\pi (s)$, i.e., \begin{equation}v_{\pi}(s) = E_\pi [G_t \, | \, s_t = s] = E_\pi \Bigg[\sum_{\tau = t + 1}^{T} \gamma^{\tau - t - 1} r_{\tau} \, \Bigg| \, s_t = s\Bigg],\end{equation} where $v_{\pi (s)$ are sometimes called the state values.}}
elements.formula-2411-036: \begin{equation}v_{\pi}(s) = E_\pi [G_t \, | \, s_t = s] = E_\pi \Bigg[\sum_{\tau = t + 1}^{T} \gamma^{\tau - t - 1} r_{\tau} \, \Bigg| \, s_t = s\Bigg],\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-192: Importantly, different policies $\pi$ lead to different state values, and there exists at least one policy $\pi^{*}$ with the highest state values: \begin{equation}v_{*}(s) = \max_{\pi} v_{\pi}(s),\end{equation} where $v_{* (s)$ are the optimal state values obtained by following one of the optimal policies $\pi^{*}$.}
elements.sent-2411-193: Similarly, the expected value of taking action $a$ in state $s$, and then follow policy $\pi$ is denoted $q_{\pi (a, s)$, i.e., \begin{equation}q_{\pi}(a, s) = E_\pi [G_t \, | \, a_t = a, s_t = s] = E_\pi \Bigg[\sum_{\tau = t + 1}^{T} \gamma^{\tau - t - 1} r_{\tau} \, \Bigg| \, a_t = a, s_t = s\Bigg],\end{equation} where $q_{\pi (a, s)$ are sometime called the Q-values.}}
elements.formula-2411-038: \begin{equation}q_{\pi}(a, s) = E_\pi [G_t \, | \, a_t = a, s_t = s] = E_\pi \Bigg[\sum_{\tau = t + 1}^{T} \gamma^{\tau - t - 1} r_{\tau} \, \Bigg| \, a_t = a, s_t = s\Bigg],\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-194: Importantly, different policies $\pi$ lead to different Q-values, and there exists at least one policy $\pi^{*}$ with the highest Q-values: \begin{equation}q_{*}(a, s) = \max_{\pi} q_{\pi}(a, s),\end{equation} where $q_{* (a, s)$ are the optimal Q-values obtained by following one of the optimal policies $\pi^{*}$.}
elements.sent-2411-195: As shown in Appendix F, the following relationship holds: \begin{equation}q_{*}(a_t, s_t) = \sum_{s_{t+1}, r_{t+1}} P(s_{t+1}, r_{t+1} | s_t, a_t) \big[ r_{t + 1} + \gamma v_{*}(s_{t+1}) \big] \label{eq:optimal-q-values-and-state-values} = \sum_{s_{t+1}, r_{t+1}} P(s_{t+1}, r_{t+1} | s_t, a_t) \big[ r_{t + 1} + \gamma \max_{a_{t+1}} q_{*}(a_{t+1}, s_{t+1}) \big], \label{eq:optimal-q-values-and-q-values}\end{equation} where intuitively Equation \eqref{eq:optimal-q-values-and-state-values says that the optimal Q-values is equal to the expected immediate reward, plus the discounted value of the future state.}

elements.sent-2411-209: In this section, we validate our approach on a maze solving task.
elements.sent-2411-210: The agent is able to move ``up'', ``down'', ``left'' and ``right'', but if the action selected would lead the agent to bump into a wall, then the agent does not move.
elements.sent-2411-211: As illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:mazes , the agent needs to navigate from the initial position (represented by the mouse) to the goal state (represented by the cheese).}
elements.sent-2411-212: Then, when the goal state has been reached, the agent must perform the action ``eat''.
elements.sent-2411-213: We study the performance of our agent based on two criteria: (i) its ability to learn the structure of various mazes, and (ii) its ability to solve the mazes by getting to the goal state and eating the cheese.
elements.sent-2411-214: First, we focus on the model's ability to learn the maze structure.
elements.sent-2411-215: By manual inspection of the learned components and transition matrices, we identified the cells which have been learnt properly (see green and orange cells in Figure \ref{fig:mazes ), and the cells that were not learned (see red cells in Figure \ref{fig:mazes ).}}
elements.sent-2411-216: Importantly, as the number of states increase, some components became unstable, effectively taking over the data points corresponding to neighbouring cells, the impacted cells are drawn in orange.
elements.sent-2411-217: Second, we looked at which mazes can be solved by our approach.
elements.sent-2411-218: We found that mazes in Figure \ref{fig:maze_8 , \ref{fig:maze_20 , \ref{fig:maze_25 , and \ref{fig:maze_26 , can be solved by our approach.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-219: However, the agent was unable to solve the maze in Figure \ref{fig:maze_10 , because of a lack of exploration, leading to an inability to learn the entire maze structure.}
elements.sent-2411-220: Similarly, the maze of Figure \ref{fig:maze_27 remained unsolved as the large number of states rendered the Gaussian components unstable, leading to some components being unlearned as time passes.}
elements.sent-2411-221: Another interesting question is how fast can the TGM agent learn to solve each maze.
elements.sent-2411-222: To answer this question, we rely on an empirical comparison between TGM and two reinforcement learning algorithms, i.e., Deep Q-network (DQN) and Advantage Actor Critic (A2C).
elements.sent-2411-223: Put simply, the DQN agent \citep{Mnih2015 learns the value of taking each action in each state, while A2C \citep{mnih2016asynchronous is a policy-based approach equipped with a critic network.}}
elements.sent-2411-224: Note, DQN, A2C and PPO, all learn a mapping from states to actions (or values) without creating a model of the environment, i.e., they are model-free approaches.
elements.sent-2411-225: Figure \ref{fig:reward_mazes illustrates the average episodic reward gathered by the TGM, DQN and A2C agents on the corresponding mazes of Figure \ref{fig:mazes .}}

elements.sent-2411-236: To conclude, we showed that the TGM agent is competitive with reinforcement learning benchmark such as DQN and A2C.
elements.sent-2411-237: More precisely, TGM outperforms A2C on all the tested mazes, except one where all agents failed to solve the task.
elements.sent-2411-238: This may be because TGM is more stable than A2C as illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:reward_maze_20 to \ref{fig:reward_maze_26 .}}
elements.sent-2411-239: Similarly, TGM performed better than DQN on one maze, similarly to DQN on three mazes, and worse than DQN on two mazes.
elements.sent-2411-240: However, while TGM sometimes performs worse than DQN, it learns an interpretable model of the environment, which can be desirable for some applications.
elements.sent-2411-241: Additionally, this model of the environment is composed of hidden states that may be useful to develop more advanced exploration strategies, which aim to explore rarely visited states.
elements.sent-2411-242: In this paper, we tackled the problem of learning the model structure from data, when the observations are continuous.
elements.sent-2411-243: More specifically, we aimed at identifying the number of clusters present in the data, and each cluster was associated to a latent state.
elements.sent-2411-244: As more data is gathered by the agent, new clusters are discovered, and the model needs to increase its number of states.
elements.sent-2411-245: In contrast, if the agent currently believes that there are more states, than the actual number of clusters in the data, then the number of states needs to decrease.
elements.sent-2411-246: We proposed a variational Gaussian mixture model, in which the Gaussian mixture model is able to remove clusters not supported by the data, and the agent is constantly monitoring the data searching for new clusters.
elements.sent-2411-247: When new clusters are identified, the corresponding components are added to the mixture.
elements.sent-2411-248: These two mechanics allow the model to learn the number of components.
elements.sent-2411-249: While this is happening, the agent also learns the parameters of the individual components through variational inference.
elements.sent-2411-250: However, inferring the current state is only part of the story, and the agent also needs to learn how different actions impact the temporal transition between states.
elements.sent-2411-251: This is done by taking advantage of a categorical-Dirichlet model, which effectively keeps track of the number of times the agent moved from a state to another when performing a specific action.
elements.sent-2411-252: Once the agent can perform inference and predict the consequences of its action, the last requirement is to perform decision making and planning.
elements.sent-2411-253: We proposed a variant of the Q-learning algorithm, which accommodates stochastic states, i.e., having beliefs over states instead of observable states.
elements.sent-2411-254: This new algorithm enabled the agent to solve several mazes, by reaching the exit position and eating the cheese.
elements.sent-2411-255: Experimentally, our approach was able to solve several mazes, but still has limitations.
elements.sent-2411-256: For example, the approach is susceptible to unlearning existing components, where several components fuse together into a single component.
elements.sent-2411-257: Additionally, since $\epsilon$-greedy is used to trade-off exploration and exploitation, the agent tends to struggle to explore remote parts of the maze.
elements.sent-2411-258: These limitations suggest that more research is required to increase the stability of the Gaussian components.
elements.sent-2411-259: Additionally, the agent would benefit from improving the exploration strategy, for example, the agent should probably focus on discovering rarely explored parts of the maze instead of randomly selecting action.
elements.sent-2411-260: Also, the environments being modelled were somewhat idealised, e.g., the organism follows a rather stereotyped trajectory, passing from one cluster to another.
elements.sent-2411-261: Furthermore, one could investigate alternative approaches for learning the number of components such as the infinite Gaussian mixture model \citep{rasmussen1999infinite .}
elements.sent-2411-262: Another interesting direction of research would be to apply the temporal Gaussian mixture to study the latent representation learned in deep active inference \citep{DeepAIwithMCMC,catal2020learning,deconstructing_DAI,DeepAI,sancaktar2020endtoend,DAI_HR,DAI_HR2,DAI_POMDP , or similarly to use the variational Gaussian mixture to study the representation learned by variational auto-encoders \citep{VAE,beta-VAE,Kingma2013,Rezende2014,bai2022gaussian,dilokthanakul2016deep ..
elements.section-2411-008: Appendix A: notation and important properties
elements.sent-2411-263: In this paper, we made extensive use of several definitions and properties, which are summarized in this appendix.
elements.sent-2411-264: Appendix A.1 provides the definition of various probability distributions, Appendix A.2 presents properties related to probability theory, and Appendix A.3 highlights properties from linear algebra.
elements.sent-2411-265: The probability density function (PDF) of a multivariate Gaussian distribution with mean $\bm{\mu}$ and precision matrix $\bm{\Lambda}$ is given by: \begin{equation}N(x; \mu, \Lambda^{-1}) = (2\pi)^{-\frac{K}{2}} |\Lambda^{-1}|^{-\frac{1}{2}} exp\Big( -\frac{1}{2} (x - \mu)^\top \Lambda (x - \mu) \Big). \label{eq:gaussian_distribution}\end{equation} The PDF of a Wishart distribution with $v$ degrees of freedom and scale matrix $\bm{W}$ is: \begin{equation}W(\Lambda; W, v) = 2^{-\frac{vK}{2}} |W|^{-\frac{v}{2}} \Gamma_K(\tfrac{v}{2})^{-1} |\Lambda|^{\frac{(v-K-1)}{2}} exp\Big( -\frac{1}{2} tr(W^{-1}\Lambda) \Big). \label{eq:wishart_distribution_def}\end{equation} The PDF of a Dirichlet distribution with concentration parameters $d$ is defined as follows: \begin{equation}Dir(D; d) = \frac{1}{B(d)}\prod_{k=1}^K D^{d_k - 1}_k. \label{eq:dirichlet_distribution_def}\end{equation} In the above equation, $\bm{D}$ was a vector.
elements.sent-2411-266: We define a Dirichlet distribution over a 3d-tensor $\bm{B}$, as a product of Dirichlet distributions over the columns of the matrices $\bm{B [a]$.}
elements.sent-2411-267: More precisely, the PDF of a Dirichlet distribution with concentration parameters $b$ is defined as: \begin{equation}Dir(B; b) = \prod_{a=1}^A \prod_{j=1}^K Dir(B[a]_{\bigcdot j}; b[a]_{\bigcdot j}) = \prod_{a=1}^A \prod_{j=1}^K \frac{1}{B(b[a]_{\bigcdot j})}\prod_{k=1}^K B[a]^{b[a]_{kj} - 1}_{kj}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-050: \begin{equation}Dir(B; b) = \prod_{a=1}^A \prod_{j=1}^K Dir(B[a]_{\bigcdot j}; b[a]_{\bigcdot j}) = \prod_{a=1}^A \prod_{j=1}^K \frac{1}{B(b[a]_{\bigcdot j})}\prod_{k=1}^K B[a]^{b[a]_{kj} - 1}_{kj}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-268: The probability mass function (PMF) of a categorical distribution with parameters $\bm{D}$ is given by: \begin{equation}Cat(z_n | D) = \prod_{k=1}^K D_{k}^{z_{nk}}. \label{eq:categorical_distribution_def}\end{equation} In the above equation, $\bm{D}$ is a vector, and the categorical distribution is a distribution over a single random variable.
elements.sent-2411-269: We extend the definition of categorical distributions to conditional distributions, by using 3d-tensor of parameters $\bm{B}$.
elements.sent-2411-270: More precisely, the PMF of a conditional categorical distribution with parameters $\bm{B}$ is: \begin{equation}Cat(z^{\tau + 1}_n | z^{\tau}_n, a^{\tau}_n, B) = \prod_{j=1}^K \prod_{k=1}^K B[a^{\tau}_n]_{jk}^{z^{\tau}_{nk}z^{\tau + 1}_{nj}}.\end{equation}.

elements.subsection-2411-011: A.3: important properties of linear algebra
elements.sent-2411-284: Given a symmetric matrix $X$, and two vectors $a$ and $b$, the following two properties hold: \begin{equation}(a - b)^\top X (a - b) = a^\top X a -2 a^\top X b + b^\top X b \label{eq:gaussian_form_expansion} a^\top X b = b^\top X a \label{eq:quadratic_form_symmetry}\end{equation} Given a symmetric matrix $X$, $N$ vectors $a_i$, and a vector $b$, the following three properties hold: \begin{equation}\sum_{i=1}^N a_i^\top X b = N \bar{a}^\top X b, \label{eq:avg_quadratic_1_form} \sum_{i=1}^N a_i b^\top = N \bar{a} b^\top, \label{eq:avg_outter_product_1} \sum_{i=1}^N b a_i^\top = N b \bar{a}^\top, \label{eq:avg_outter_product_2}\end{equation} where $\bar{a = \frac{1 {N \sum_{i=1 ^N a_i$.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-285: Given a matrix $X$, and two vectors $a$ and $b$: \begin{equation}a^\top X b = tr(ba^\top X), \label{eq:trace_and_quadratic_form}\end{equation} where $tr(X) = \sum_i X_{ii}$ is the trace $X$.

elements.sent-2411-336: We now derive update equations for the empirical prior's parameters, and a new set of update equations for the variational posterior that relies only on the empirical prior's parameters.

elements.sent-2411-343: Focusing on $Y_4$, we have: \begin{equation}Y_4 = (\beta_k + N_k) \hat{m}_k \hat{m}_k^\top = \frac{1}{\beta_k + N_k} (\beta_k m_k + N_k \bar{x}_k)(\beta_k m_k + N_k \bar{x}_k)^\top = \frac{1}{\beta_k + N_k} (\beta_k m_k + N^{'}_k \bar{x}^{'}_k + N^{''}_k \bar{x}^{''}_k)(\beta_k m_k + N^{'}_k \bar{x}^{'}_k + N^{''}_k \bar{x}^{''}_k)^\top = \frac{1}{\bar{\beta}_k + N^{''}_k} (\bar{\beta}_k \bar{m}_k + N^{''}_k \bar{x}^{''}_k)(\bar{\beta}_k \bar{m}_k + N^{''}_k \bar{x}^{''}_k)^\top = \frac{\bar{\beta}_k^2}{\bar{\beta}_k + N^{''}_k} \bar{m}_k\bar{m}_k^\top + \frac{\bar{\beta}_k N^{''}_k}{\bar{\beta}_k + N^{''}_k} \bar{m}_k \bar{x}^{'' \top}_k + \frac{\bar{\beta}_k N^{''}_k}{\bar{\beta}_k + N^{''}_k} \bar{x}^{''}_k \bar{m}_k^\top + \frac{N^{'' 2}_k}{\bar{\beta}_k + N^{''}_k} \bar{x}^{''}_k \bar{x}^{'' \top}_k.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-347: Substituting $Y_5$ into $Y_3$, we get: \begin{equation}Y_3 = \beta_k m_k m_k^\top - \frac{\beta_k^2}{\beta_k + N^{'}_k} m_km_k^\top - \frac{\beta_k N^{'}_k}{\beta_k + N^{'}_k} m_k \bar{x}^{' \top}_k - \frac{\beta_k N^{'}_k}{\beta_k + N^{'}_k} \bar{x}^{'}_k m_k^\top - \frac{N^{' 2}_k}{\beta_k + N^{'}_k} \bar{x}^{'}_k \bar{x}^{' \top}_k + N^{'}_k\bar{x}^{'}_k \bar{x}^{' \top}_k \nonumber + \frac{\bar{\beta}_kN^{''}_k}{\bar{\beta}_k + N^{''}_k} (\bar{x}^{''}_k - \bar{m}_k)(\bar{x}^{''}_k - \bar{m}_k)^\top = \frac{\beta_k(\beta_k + N^{'}_k) - \beta_k^2}{\beta_k + N^{'}_k} m_km_k^\top - \frac{\beta_k N^{'}_k}{\beta_k + N^{'}_k} m_k \bar{x}^{' \top}_k - \frac{\beta_k N^{'}_k}{\beta_k + N^{'}_k} \bar{x}^{'}_k m_k^\top + \frac{N^{'}_k(\beta_k + N^{'}_k) - N^{' 2}_k}{\beta_k + N^{'}_k} \bar{x}^{'}_k \bar{x}^{' \top}_k \nonumber + \frac{\bar{\beta}_kN^{''}_k}{\bar{\beta}_k + N^{''}_k} (\bar{x}^{''}_k - \bar{m}_k)(\bar{x}^{''}_k - \bar{m}_k)^\top = \frac{\beta_kN^{'}_k}{\beta_k + N^{'}_k} [m_km_k^\top - m_k \bar{x}^{' \top}_k - \bar{x}^{'}_k m_k^\top + \bar{x}^{'}_k \bar{x}^{' \top}_k] + \frac{\bar{\beta}_kN^{''}_k}{\bar{\beta}_k + N^{''}_k} (\bar{x}^{''}_k - \bar{m}_k)(\bar{x}^{''}_k - \bar{m}_k)^\top = \frac{\beta_kN^{'}_k}{\beta_k + N^{'}_k} (\bar{x}^{'}_k - m_k)(\bar{x}^{'}_k - m_k)^\top + \frac{\bar{\beta}_kN^{''}_k}{\bar{\beta}_k + N^{''}_k} (\bar{x}^{''}_k - \bar{m}_k)(\bar{x}^{''}_k - \bar{m}_k)^\top.\end{equation}.

elements.subsection-2411-028: E.2 Empirical prior, update equation for $\boldmath{B}
elements.sent-2411-355: In this section, we re-arrange this result to obtain an update equation for the empirical prior parameters, and another update equation that expresses the variational posterior parameters as a function of the empirical prior parameters.
elements.sent-2411-356: More precisely, we show that the parameters of $E^*(\bm{B )$ should be computed as follows: \begin{equation}\bar{b}[a]_{kj} = b[a]_{kj} + \sum_{n \in M^{'}} [a = a_n^0] \bm{\hat{r}}_{nk}^0 \bm{\hat{r}}_{nj}^1.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-357: Similarly, the parameters of $Q^*(\bm{B )$ should be computed as follows: \begin{equation}\hat{b}[a]_{kj} = \bar{b}[a]_{kj} + \sum_{n \in M^{''}} [a = a_n^0] \bm{\hat{r}}_{nk}^0 \bm{\hat{r}}_{nj}^1.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-358: Importantly, since $M^{' \cup M^{'' = \{1, ..., M\}$, we can re-arrange the update equation for the variational parameters as follows: \begin{equation}\hat{b}[a]_{kj} = b[a]_{kj} + \sum_{n=1}^M [a = a_n^0] \bm{\hat{r}}_{nk}^0\bm{\hat{r}}_{nk}^1 = \underbrace{b[a]_{kj} + \sum_{n \in M^{'}} [a = a_n^0] \bm{\hat{r}}_{nk}^0 \bm{\hat{r}}_{nj}^1}_{\bar{b}[a]_{kj}} + \sum_{n \in M^{''}} [a = a_n^0] \bm{\hat{r}}_{nk}^0 \bm{\hat{r}}_{nj}^1,\end{equation} where because the parameters of the empirical prior should take into account the information of the data points indexed by $M^{'}$, we have: \begin{equation}\bar{b}[a]_{kj} = b[a]_{kj} + \sum_{n \in M^{'}} [a = a_n^0] \bm{\hat{r}}_{nk}^0 \bm{\hat{r}}_{nj}^1,\end{equation} and the parameters of the variational posterior can be written as: \begin{equation}\hat{b}[a]_{kj} = \bar{b}[a]_{kj} + \sum_{n \in M^{''}} [a = a_n^0] \bm{\hat{r}}_{nk}^0 \bm{\hat{r}}_{nj}^1.\end{equation}. \end{mdframed.}}}
